rationale: The citing paper centers on curiosity/uncertainty resolution and affective-forecasting errors, and the cited paper also provides affective-forecasting evidence (planned vs. actual behavior after gains/losses), so a nontrivial theoretical link is plausible. However, the available evidence is only a DOI-level match with no in-text citation context or stated role of this specific reference in the citing paper. Given this sparse evidence, it is not safe to drop but also not strong enough to clearly keep.
